evidence: Anna acknowledged this and mentioned she wants to do less of the logistics work this year, asking if Vrijen would take over more of the event coordination responsibilities. Vrijen agreed enthusiastically.
Anna concluded by mentioning being okay with meeting with Starbridge next week.

Vrijen described himself as becoming a '1% or 0.1% power user' of products he embeds with—someone who dives so deep into a platform that he reaches functionality most users never discover. Yet he simultaneously identifies as 'non-technical but increasingly trying to push my boundaries.'

Is there a fundamental mismatch between how we define 'technical' vs. 'power user'? Perhaps the most sophisticated users of any system are those who think in first principles about *how* the system should work, regardless of whether they can build it themselves.

Vrijen has moved 12 cities across 33 years. Perhaps frequent relocation doesn't just make you adaptable—it makes you *good at noticing* adaptation. The people who moved often may be more practiced at recognizing when to stop trying to adapt and start trying to belong.

source_display: Human Support Systems vs Human-Centered AI
evidence: Zain's entire thesis centers on what he calls 'human support systems'—infrastructure needed to navigate an AI-automated future. There's an unexamined assumption that we're building 'human-centered AI' to help humans *compete* with AI. What if the real opportunity is building systems that help humans *let go* of the need to compete?
Vrijen's work at Careerspan and Zo both aim to help humans become better self-advocates using AI tools. Both emphasized 'collaboration over competition.'

source_display: Small World Algorithm as Trust Mechanism
evidence: The Logan connection—Zain randomly connected with Vrijen's co-founder on TikTok, and that morning during their check-in, both mentioned this 'interesting person they were talking to'—creates a 'glitch in the matrix' moment. We optimize for efficiency, but trust is created by inefficiency.

source_display: Authenticity as Defensive Moat Against Clickbait
evidence: Maybe authenticity isn't a competitive strategy—it's a *defensive* one. Authentic content creates trust slowly and transactionally, whereas clickbait extracts attention quickly and parasitically. The people with real things to say don't need to rely on cheap tactics... they're playing a different game entirely.

Zain had randomly connected with Logan (Vrijen's co-founder) on TikTok after seeing her content about work and skills. Neither realized the shared connection until Vrijen mentioned it during their call.
Vrijen will introduce Zain to FOHE leadership. FOHE is selective free group; Vrijen joining as NYC co-lead; wants to bring valuable connections.
Zain's background and interests strongly align with FOHE mission (future of higher education, mid-to-upper-tier edtech executives).

Careerspan isn't a replacement for Handshake or any recruiting platform—it's the 'missing layer' that makes their products defensible. This reframes the entire partnership narrative from competition to symbiosis.

Distribution platforms (Handshake) have solved access, but access without signal is noise. Signal technology (Careerspan) completes the puzzle. Careerspan could become the infrastructure layer for the entire university recruiting ecosystem.

Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure to capture signal but lacks distribution. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone. This isn't about revenue—it's about owning the future of talent identification.

The entire Careerspan thesis rests on the premise that behavioral signal (how people think) is more valuable than profile data (what they've done). This challenges the fundamental premise of LinkedIn and traditional resumes.

Positioning Careerspan as a strategic hedge for Handshake's declining core business ($200M, only 6% growth). Even if the partnership never becomes a major revenue line, it provides data supremacy and option value for Handshake's future direction.

The repeated emphasis on leveraging founder club networks and warm introductions reveals an implicit strategy: Careerspan is playing a relationship-heavy, founder-to-founder game rather than a traditional enterprise sales motion.

Emphasizing the 3-person team as an asset (low acquisition overhead) turns a potential weakness into strength. In an era of bloated startups with high burn rates, being lean makes you an attractive acquisition target.

The emphasis on month-on-month retention and return rates reveals a long-term data play. The real value isn't in helping someone get one job—it's in tracking their entire career trajectory and predicting future success. This creates a dataset that compounds in value over time.

Extended conversation about potential partnership with Handshake. Handshake's core recruiting business: ~$200M revenue, but only 6% growth in last year. Company is moving away from recruiting line of business.
